topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a monotonous soundtrack to my growing internal turmoil. I stared at the spreadsheet, the numbers blurring into an incomprehensible landscape. Sarah’s salary, juxtaposed with mine, felt like a physical blow. I kept glancing over at her desk, a tight knot forming in my chest with every glance. When she finally approached, her smile was bright, completely oblivious to the shadow I was casting over my own professional standing. "Hey, Mark," she chirped, pointing at a quarterly report. Her voice was light, and it made my throat feel suddenly dry. I managed a weak nod, the effort of maintaining a neutral expression draining me. Later that afternoon, I found myself meticulously re-checking every line item on my own pay stub, the paper feeling brittle under my fingertips. It was a tedious, self-punishing ritual.

[story 2]

My stomach clenched with a dull, heavy ache. I had overheard the conversation, a snippet of casual exchange that had twisted into a sharp, unwelcome truth. The realization hit me like a sudden drop in temperature. I spent the rest of the day in a fog, my usual sharp focus entirely absent. Every time I typed an email, my fingers felt clumsy, and I kept catching myself mentally comparing figures. Walking back to my cubicle, I leaned against the partition, closing my eyes for a moment. The silence amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. When I finally opened them, I felt a profound sense of inadequacy, a heavy weight settling onto my shoulders that made breathing shallow. I needed to do something, anything, to right the imbalance, but the path forward felt impossibly steep.

[story 3]

The afternoon sun slanted through the blinds, illuminating dust motes dancing in the air above my desk. I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic warm against my palms, and let the silence stretch out, thick and suffocating. The facts were undeniable, stark and unforgiving. I kept replaying the interaction in my head, the casual way she spoke of her compensation, the sheer disparity between our efforts and our rewards. A tremor ran through my hand as I gripped the mug tighter. I started organizing my digital files with an almost obsessive neatness, a desperate attempt to impose order on the chaos churning inside me. I kept glancing toward the breakroom, half-expecting her to walk by, an involuntary flinch shooting through me at the thought of confronting the reality head-on.
